definition: The smallest unit of matter that retains the chemical properties of an element. Atoms are extremely small – a grain of sand contains roughly one hundred thousand billion of them.
A positively charged particle in the nucleus of an atom. The number of protons is unique to each element and is called the atomic number – protons determine which element the atom is.

An uncharged particle in the atomic nucleus. Together with protons, neutrons form the nucleus. The number of neutrons can vary – atoms with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons are called isotopes.

A negatively charged particle that orbits the nucleus in electron shells. The electrons in the outermost shell (valence electrons) determine the chemical properties of the atom and how it forms bonds.
The number of protons in an atom's nucleus – unique to each element. Carbon always has atomic number 6, oxygen always 8. The atomic number determines where an element is placed in the periodic table.
Variants of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons and therefore different mass numbers. Carbon-12, carbon-13 and carbon-14 are all isotopes of carbon.

The electrons in the outermost electron shell of an atom. These govern how the atom reacts chemically and how it forms bonds with other atoms. Atoms strive to have a full outer shell (noble gas structure).
A table of all known elements arranged by atomic number. Rows are called periods (same number of electron shells); columns are called groups (similar chemical properties).

The elements in group 18 (helium, neon, argon, etc.) with a full outermost electron shell. Extremely stable and almost never react with other substances. Used in, for example, fluorescent tubes and light bulbs.
The metals in group 1 (lithium, sodium, potassium, etc.) with one valence electron. They react vigorously with water and form strong bases. Soft metals that oxidise easily.

The elements in group 17 (fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine, etc.) with seven valence electrons. Highly reactive and form salts when they react with metals. Used, among other things, in disinfection.
An atom that has gained an electric charge by losing or gaining electrons. Positive ions (cations) have lost electrons; negative ions (anions) have gained electrons.

A chemical bond in which positive and negative ions are attracted to each other by electrical forces. Ionic compounds form crystal lattices with high melting points. Example: sodium chloride (table salt, NaCl).
A chemical bond in which atoms share electrons to achieve a full outer shell. Also called an electron-pair bond; forms molecules. Examples: water (H₂O) and carbon dioxide (CO₂).
